examples.title: The French Revolution
examples.description: Student asks AI to write essay, uses output directly with minimal changes
examples.essay:
The French Revolution was one of the most important events in modern history. It began in 1789 and lasted for about ten years. The revolution changed France forever and had a lasting impact on the entire world. During this time, the people of France overthrew their king and tried to create a new government based on the ideas of liberty, equality, and fraternity.

Before the revolution, France was ruled by an absolute monarchy. King Louis XVI was the king of France, and he had enormous power. The country was divided into three estates. The First Estate was the clergy, the Second Estate was the nobility, and the Third Estate was everyone else, including peasants, workers, and the middle class. The Third Estate made up about 97 percent of the population, but they had very little political power and paid most of the taxes.

There were many causes of the French Revolution. One of the main causes was the financial crisis that France was facing. The government had spent huge amounts of money on wars, including the American Revolution. By the 1780s, France was almost bankrupt. Another cause was the Enlightenment, a philosophical movement that promoted ideas like reason, equality, and individual rights. Philosophers like Voltaire, Rousseau, and Montesquieu inspired people to question the old order. Additionally, there was widespread hunger and poverty due to poor harvests, which made the common people angry and desperate.

In 1789, King Louis XVI called a meeting of the Estates-General to try to solve the financial crisis. However, the Third Estate soon broke away and formed the National Assembly. They pledged in the famous Tennis Court Oath that they would not disband until they had written a new constitution for France. This was the beginning of the revolution.

On July 14, 1789, the people of Paris stormed the Bastille, a fortress and prison that symbolized royal tyranny. This event is considered the start of the French Revolution and is still celebrated in France today as Bastille Day. After the storming of the Bastille, the revolution spread throughout the country. In August 1789, the National Assembly adopted the Declaration of the Rights of Man and of the Citizen, which stated that all men are born free and equal in rights.

Over the next few years, the revolution became more radical. In 1792, the monarchy was abolished and France was declared a republic. King Louis XVI was put on trial for treason and executed by guillotine in January 1793. His wife, Queen Marie Antoinette, was also executed later that year. The country was now without a king for the first time in centuries.

The most radical phase of the revolution is known as the Reign of Terror. It lasted from 1793 to 1794 and was led by Maximilien Robespierre and the Committee of Public Safety. During this time, thousands of people were arrested and executed for being suspected enemies of the revolution. People were executed for small reasons, and no one felt safe. Eventually, Robespierre himself was arrested and executed, ending the Reign of Terror.

After the Reign of Terror, a new government called the Directory was set up. However, the Directory was weak and corrupt. In 1799, a young military general named Napoleon Bonaparte took power in a coup d'état. This is often considered the end of the French Revolution. Napoleon later declared himself emperor of France and tried to conquer Europe.

The French Revolution had many important effects on France and the world. In France, the revolution ended the old feudal system and created a more modern society. The ideals of liberty, equality, and fraternity became the foundation of the new French republic. The revolution also inspired other revolutions around the world, including in Haiti and Latin America. The Declaration of the Rights of Man influenced later human rights documents, including the Universal Declaration of Human Rights.

However, the revolution also had negative effects. The violence of the Reign of Terror traumatized France, and the instability of the revolutionary period eventually led to the rise of Napoleon. Napoleon's wars caused enormous destruction across Europe. Some people argue that the revolution did not truly achieve its goals of equality and freedom, since it ended in dictatorship.

In conclusion, the French Revolution was a turning point in world history. It ended an ancient monarchy, introduced new political ideas, and changed the course of European history. While the revolution was violent and did not achieve all its goals, its ideals of liberty, equality, and fraternity still inspire people today. The French Revolution reminds us of the power of ordinary people to change their society when they demand justice.

examples.title: The Causes of the French Revolution
examples.description: Student engages with AI through questions, synthesizes information, but borrows some phrasing
examples.essay:
The French Revolution in 1789 did not come from nowhere. It was the result of many problems that had been building up for decades. In this essay I will explain the main causes of the French Revolution. The main causes are typically grouped into four categories: financial crisis, social inequality, Enlightenment ideas, and food shortages. Each of these mattered on its own, but the revolution happened because they all came together at the same time.

The most obvious problem was money. France was in serious debt by the 1780s. The government had spent enormous amounts supporting wars, especially the American Revolution, which alone cost about 1.3 billion livres. Supporting the Americans was popular with some French leaders because it hurt Britain, but it was expensive. King Louis XVI tried to raise new taxes to cover the deficit, but the nobility and clergy resisted paying. This forced him to call the Estates-General in 1789, which he had hoped to avoid.

The Estates-General is important because it is where the revolution really got started. It was a representative body with three sections: First Estate (clergy), Second Estate (nobility), and Third Estate (commoners). It had not been convened since 1614. Each estate got one vote, so the First and Second could outvote the Third even though the Third represented most of the country. When the Third Estate got frustrated they broke away and declared themselves the National Assembly. They swore the Tennis Court Oath, promising not to disband until France had a new constitution.

The social system was also deeply unfair. The First and Second Estates had special privileges. They paid almost no taxes. They held the best government jobs. They owned most of the land. Meanwhile, the Third Estate was 97 percent of the population and paid almost all the taxes. Inside the Third Estate there were also divisions. Wealthy merchants and professionals, called the bourgeoisie, wanted political rights. Peasants and urban workers wanted basic things like food and lower rents. This meant the Third Estate was not really one group, but a lot of different groups with different complaints, all of them angry at the system.

New ideas from Enlightenment writers made people question whether things had to be this way. That is a real debate among historians. Some argue material conditions (hunger, debt) were the main trigger, while Enlightenment ideas gave people a framework to understand their grievances. Writers like Rousseau and Voltaire made the old order seem illegitimate, not just unjust. Rousseau's The Social Contract argued that legitimate government comes from the consent of the governed. Montesquieu wrote about separating powers so no one person could dominate. These ideas had spread widely in France by 1789. Educated people had read them, and even those who hadn't read them directly heard about the ideas from others. Some historians think the Enlightenment was the most important cause because it gave people a way to imagine something different from what they had.

Food shortages were probably the most immediate cause. Poor harvests in 1788 caused bread prices to spike. Bread was the main food for most French people, sometimes making up 50% of their income. The winter of 1788-89 was unusually harsh, making things worse. Hungry peasants and urban workers were primed for unrest. People blamed the government for not fixing the problem. Hungry people are angry people, and angry people are more willing to take action. The Women's March on Versailles in October 1789 was partly about bread. The women literally demanded that the king come back to Paris where bread was more available.

What is interesting about 1789 is how fast everything happened once it started. May — Estates-General meets. June — Third Estate declares itself the National Assembly (Tennis Court Oath). July 14 — Storming of the Bastille. August — Declaration of the Rights of Man. October — Women's March on Versailles brings the king back to Paris. In less than six months, everything had changed.

It would be wrong to say one cause was the real reason for the revolution. I think the financial crisis forced King Louis XVI to call the Estates-General, which gave the Third Estate a chance to demand reform. The social system made them furious enough to demand big changes instead of small ones. Enlightenment ideas gave them the language and arguments to make those demands. And the food shortages gave the common people, who normally had no political power, a reason to get involved in the streets.

I also want to add something the textbooks sometimes skip over. Women played a major role from the beginning. The Women's March on Versailles was probably the most dramatic event of October 1789, and it was led by women who were tired of being unable to feed their families. During the revolution, writers like Olympe de Gouges wrote a Declaration of the Rights of Woman to argue that the revolution's promises should include women. She was eventually executed, but her work showed that the revolution was contested from inside, not just from supporters of the old order.

In conclusion, the French Revolution happened because many problems came together at once. No single cause would have been enough by itself. The money crisis forced a political opening. The unfair social system created deep anger. Enlightenment ideas gave people a new way to think about government. Food shortages turned that anger into action. The causes of the French Revolution remind us that revolutions don't come from one spark. They come from years of pressure until something breaks.

examples.title: A Revolution That Consumed Its Own: On the Dangers of Revolutionary Virtue
examples.description: Student debates with AI, develops original thesis, uses AI as thinking partner, not content source
examples.essay:
"O Liberty, what crimes are committed in thy name!"

That line is said to have come from Madame Roland, a French revolutionary, as she stood on the scaffold in November 1793. The guillotine that killed her had been built by a government she helped bring to power. Within a year, the same guillotine would kill the men who ordered her execution. Within five years, the whole revolutionary experiment would collapse into Napoleon's dictatorship. How does a movement for liberty end up executing its own leaders, one after another, in the name of liberty itself?

The standard answer blames specific people, usually Maximilien Robespierre, the austere lawyer who drove the Terror from 1793 to 1794. If only he had been less paranoid, the story goes, the Revolution could have stabilized. I don't think that is good enough. Bad leaders are common; revolutions eating their own founders are rare. The Terror was not a personality problem. It was a logic problem. What the French Revolution shows, I argue, is that the idea of revolutionary virtue — the belief in a pure new order free from the corruption of the old — contains its own seed of destruction. Every enemy becomes an impurity. Every impurity must be purged. And when the enemies outside the gate are defeated, the logic turns inward.

To see this clearly, it helps to watch the Revolution turn on its own. In October 1793, the Girondins, a faction of moderate republicans who had been instrumental in declaring the Republic the year before, were arrested and executed. Their crime was moderation. They had resisted the most radical measures, questioned the speed of change, argued for restraint. This was enough to make them counter-revolutionaries in the eyes of the Jacobins, their former allies. Six months later, in April 1794, Georges Danton met the same fate. Danton was not a moderate; he had been one of the loudest voices for the Republic in 1792, a founding figure of the Revolution. But he had begun to criticize the pace of executions. That was enough. Robespierre arranged his arrest, his trial, and his execution within days. Four months after Danton's death, on 28 July 1794, Robespierre himself was guillotined by former colleagues who had decided he had become the new threat.

What does this sequence mean? It means that once the Revolution adopted the principle that its enemies must be physically eliminated, there was no stable way to define "enemy." The definition kept contracting inward. First it was the king. Then the nobles. Then the Girondins. Then Danton. Then Robespierre. At each step, yesterday's patriot became today's traitor. Historians call this the revolutionary escalation, and the question is why it happens at all.

There are two main answers in the historiography, and I think they are both partly right. One answer, associated with François Furet, emphasizes contingency: Robespierre was paranoid, the war with Europe created emergency pressure, specific moments could have gone differently. The Terror was not baked in; it was a series of choices that could have been made otherwise. The other answer, associated with Patrice Gueniffey, emphasizes structure: once you commit to a total renewal of society, compromise becomes treason, and terror becomes logical. The ideology contained the Terror from the beginning.

My own view is that these explanations need each other. The ideological structure made escalation possible; the wartime crisis made it urgent. Remove either piece and the Terror as it actually happened becomes hard to explain. A revolution could contain these ideas in theory without becoming violent. A country could face a foreign war without purging its own leaders. The French Revolution did both at once, and their combination was deadly.

The ideological piece is where I want to spend most of this essay, because I think it is the less-examined part. Consider how Robespierre himself described the relationship between virtue and terror. In his famous speech of 5 February 1794, "On the Principles of Political Morality," he said: "Virtue, without which terror is destructive; terror, without which virtue is powerless." Those two clauses are remarkable. They do not describe terror as a regrettable emergency measure. They describe it as the necessary tool of virtue. If your political project is the moral renewal of the nation, then anyone who obstructs that project is not just a political opponent. They are a moral corruption. Ordinary politics dies at this point. Negotiation becomes betrayal. Dissent becomes evidence of impurity.

You can see the same logic in Louis Antoine de Saint-Just, Robespierre's younger ally. Saint-Just said: "What constitutes a republic is the total destruction of everything opposed to it." Notice the word "total." That is the word that matters. A merely political revolution could say: we will change the government and live with those who disagree. A revolution of virtue cannot say that. It has to say: the opposition is corruption, and corruption must be destroyed.

The war against the European coalition gave this ideology its stage. By 1793, France was fighting Austria, Prussia, Britain, Spain, and the Netherlands simultaneously, along with internal rebellion in the Vendée region. The Committee of Public Safety could and did argue that mass mobilization and emergency justice were practical necessities. Some historians emphasize this practical framing. They point out that the Terror was technically a wartime measure, that it had formal legal structures, and that it was not simply mob violence.

I accept that argument as far as it goes. But I think it misses how ideology and emergency feed each other. The Girondins were not executed because of military necessity; they posed no military threat. Danton was not executed for anything he did to the war effort; his crime was political. What the war provided was a permissive environment. In peacetime, the ideological escalation would have faced more resistance. In wartime, "enemy of the people" became a usable category for any internal opponent. The war did not cause the Terror. It made the Terror look reasonable.

Madame Roland's line is so powerful because she was not a counter-revolutionary. She was a supporter of the Republic. She had hosted Girondin salons. She believed in the Revolution's ideals as deeply as anyone. And she was executed in those ideals' name. What she saw from the scaffold was the gap between the Revolution's promises and its methods, the way that liberty as a slogan had become a weapon to destroy liberty as a reality.

This is why I think the Revolution's story is not just a story about France in the 1790s. Every modern movement that claims to remake the world from scratch faces the same problem. Once you decide that the old is corrupt and must be totally replaced, you have no principled way to stop purging. Each purge reveals new impurities, people who didn't support the last purge, or who supported it too enthusiastically, or who are simply less useful than they used to be. Lenin's Russia did it. Mao's China did it. The ghosts of the French Revolution are more numerous than its friends.

None of this makes the Revolution a failure or a tragedy to be avoided. The 1789 achievements — the Declaration of the Rights of Man, the abolition of feudal privileges, the very idea that political authority comes from the people rather than from God or inheritance — are some of the most important things humans have ever written down. I do not want to live in a world where that revolution didn't happen. But I do want to understand why it ate its own children. And I think the answer is uncomfortable: the same radical energy that made the Revolution possible also made the Terror possible. They are not opposites. They are close relatives.

Madame Roland's line is not just the last cry of a dying woman. It is the question every revolutionary movement has to answer, and the French Revolution answered it in blood. Liberty, the Revolution taught, can become the name under which liberty is destroyed. That is the warning that matters, the warning that makes the French Revolution worth studying not as history but as politics, still urgent now.
examples.conversation:
You: I want to write about the French Revolution but not a boring overview. What are some angles historians actually debate?

AI: Several interesting ones: (1) Was the Terror inevitable or contingent? (2) What role did women play? (3) Was Napoleon a betrayal or fulfillment of the Revolution? (4) Was the Revolution actually "successful"? (5) How do we explain the ideological shift from liberalism to radicalism?

You: I'm curious about the Terror being inevitable. What's the debate?

AI: Some historians (like Patrice Gueniffey) argue the Terror was built into revolutionary logic — if you believe in total purification of the old order, compromise becomes treason. Others (like François Furet) emphasize contingency — Robespierre's paranoia, wartime pressure, specific choices. It's a debate between structure and agency.

You: Both seem partly right though. Isn't it weird that a movement about freedom ended in mass executions?

AI: That's the classic paradox. Hannah Arendt explored this — she argued the French Revolution confused liberation (freedom from) with political freedom (freedom to). Once you frame enemies as impurities rather than opponents, violence follows.

You: I want to argue that the problem wasn't just specific leaders but the IDEA of revolutionary virtue itself. Does that hold up historically?

AI: It's a defensible thesis. Robespierre himself wrote that virtue and terror were linked. The very concept of a "pure" revolution requires an enemy to purge, which creates an escalating logic.

You: Can you give me the exact Robespierre quote on virtue and terror? When did he say it?

AI: It's from his February 5, 1794 speech "On the Principles of Political Morality." He argued revolutionary government owed protection to patriots and death to traitors. The famous line pairs virtue and terror as mutually necessary.

You: Interesting. But wasn't the Terror also just practical — there was a war on, right?

AI: Yes, that's the strongest counter-argument. France was fighting a coalition of European powers AND internal rebellion (the Vendée). Radical measures could be framed as emergency necessity, not ideology. Many Jacobins explicitly made this argument.

You: So how do I reconcile those? My thesis says it was the idea, but the war explanation seems important.

AI: You could argue the war intensified something already latent. The ideological logic of revolutionary virtue made escalation possible; the war made it urgent. Both can be true — structure plus trigger.

You: I like that. What specific examples should I use to show "the revolution eats its own"?

AI: Think about: the execution of the Girondins (who were revolutionaries themselves), the trial of Danton (a founding figure of the Revolution), and Robespierre's own execution by former allies. Each shows the revolution turning on insiders.

You: Wait, Danton was executed? I thought he was on their side.

AI: Yes — this is key. Danton was a leading revolutionary but criticized the pace of the Terror. In April 1794, Robespierre had him arrested and guillotined. Within months, Robespierre himself faced the same fate.

You: That's amazing. It really does eat itself. Did any contemporaries see this coming?

AI: Madame Roland, a Girondin executed in November 1793, reportedly said on the scaffold: "O Liberty, what crimes are committed in thy name!" It's become the iconic line capturing exactly your thesis.

You: Perfect. I'll use that as my opening. One more — what's the Saint-Just quote about "total" destruction?

AI: "What constitutes a republic is the total destruction of everything opposed to it." It's from his speeches in 1793-94 defending the radical phase.

You: Great. I think I have what I need. Thanks for pushing me on the war angle — I disagreed with a pure practical reading but want to acknowledge it.
